item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wish I had this when I wrote my undergrad thesis (Economics major here). Take my +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After a very recent dust-up with some an-caps (#SecondCivilWar), I read the first culture report and was like "Oh, I've never seen a writer with a political agenda use this site as their soapbox, but I guess they had to show up eventually" and started to reach for the downvote.

Then I thought "Not everyone is an idiot and it's not fair to vote based on my political beliefs". So I kept reading.

Eventually I realized that "Human Spirit" just meant "How optimistic are we being, here?" and with that my reading adjusted appropriately.

What I'm trying to say is bravo, this is a well researched and we'll written commentary. Thank you for this journey.
+1

Addendum:

After all that talk I almost forgot to actually go back and +1 the damn thing. It's been done.

Addendum 2:

You should probably remove the disclaimer from your Author Comment. Your work makes a stellar show of itself and you only undercut your work when you say "this isn't meant as a full critique"

You did a brilliant job and clearly put a lot of research into it. Take pride in a well-executed piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Probably would have liked this more if it engaged more deeply with the political philosophies described. A certain amount of condensing/exaggeration is necessary in a short format like the SCP article, but this just felt oversimplified. And silly; the obvious reference to ancap memes rubbed me the wrong way, for instance.

Might also have liked this more if some more exotic, less well-known political systems were included, rather than those which happen to be most fashionable or notorious on the web at the moment.

An excellent concept, though.

edit: I think this idea was always going to be difficult to pull off effectively, given how politically passionate society at large, and the SCP community in particular, are these days. You have to give at least some subjective interpretation of how each of these systems would pan out, and that's inevitably going to piss some people off – either because you haven't been favourable enough to their chosen system, or harsh enough to their bete noire!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanted to like this, but the execution felt really bland. Those last notes didn't mesh with the rest of the article and just seemed like tacked-on danger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SimCity Master Class level. The experiment logs were interesting with a dash of fridge horror. All those simulated people living and dying by the algorithms.

item.title: Re: Disclaimer and thanks
item.content:encoded:
Seconding the above on all fronts, except that in my case that perfect ending turned a reluctant downvote into a neutral vote.

Definitely think that the logs are a big part of the appeal here; I'll throw in a suggestion to explore things like the Horseshoe Effect1 and non-human factors like resource availability and environmental hazards. Say, set everything up for "mixed-market economy", then crank up "hazardous wildlife" and "accessible natural resources" a few notches to get Australia.

Another good example is the Amazon Rainforest: It turns out the region is littered with networks of paved road and abandoned cities, and that quite a bit of the forest itself may actually be the remnants of carefully managed forest gardens. They had a good thing going until smallpox hit them like the plague hit Europe about a hundred years earlier, and there weren't enough people to manage the forests: Cue Mad Max-grade societal collapse, but unlike in Europe their agricultural system went feral instead of turning into empty fields, making it harder to come back from.

Footnotes
1. ie, badly managed capitalism and badly managed communism are hard to tell apart

item.title: Re: Disclaimer and thanks
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure how I feel about this. I like the general idea of an economic based SCP as something interesting that hasn't been done much, but the specific execution feels a bit bland, being a machine where you pull levers and stuff magically happens. I'm also wondering why only the human spirit setting was changed in the test logs - perhaps testing with other variables would spice it up.

The punchline made me laugh enough to +1 though.

item.title: Disclaimer and thanks
item.content:encoded:
Disclaimer: This is not meant to be a serious critique or endorsment of any political/econmic system. This is a silly internet article I wrote for fun and which is meant to be a satirical take on how any system can turn out very wrong because of human nature.

Big thanks to OverThinking, MaliceAforethought, Zyn and TinyStubbornLights for the critique of this article. Happy New Year everyone!
